Data Visualization / Group Project 8(A)

Alicia Mañas, Paula Artiz and Lidia Sánchez

2026-03-5

Part A

1. Where and why was the information collected?

# The information was extracted from ancient DNA samples cataloged in Table S1 of the 2018 Science article “The evolutionary history of dogs in the Americas” by Máire Ní Leathlobhair and other authors (2018).

# Reference: Ní Leathlobhair, M., Perri, A. R., Irving-Pease, E. K., Witt, K. E., Linderholm, A., Haile, J., Lebrasseur, O., Ameen, C., Blick, J., Boyko, A. R., Brace, S., Cortes, Y. N., Crockford, S. J., Devault, A., Dimopoulos, E. A., Eldridge, M., Enk, J., Gopalakrishnan, S., … Frantz, L. A. F. (2018). The evolutionary history of dogs in the Americas. Science, 361(6397), 81–85. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.aao4776

2. Which is the meaning of each variable?

Name Definition
Extract_ID Unique internal code assigned to the DNA extraction.
Depository_ID Inventory number from the museum or institution where the physical remains are stored.
Dog/Wolf Status Taxonomic classification of the species.
Country Modern-day country where the sample was excavated.
Site name Specific archaeological or paleontological site of origin.
Lat / Long Geographic coordinates (Latitude and Longitude) of the find site.
Analysed Nuclear Shows if the sample was used for nuclear genomic analysis (1 = Yes, 0 = No)
mtDNA Analysed Shows if the mitochondrial DNA was included in the analysis (1 = Yes, 0 = No).
mtDNA capture Shows if enrichment methods were used to pull out mtDNA sequences (1 = Yes, 0 = No).
Radiocarbon Lab Code of the laboratory that performed the study.
Direct date? ‘Yes’ if the date comes from the specimen itself; ‘No’ if the date is inferred from associated materials.
Radiocarbon date (95.4% interval) The calibrated date range before present (BP) at a 2-sigma confidence level.
Age Estimated age based on the archaeological layer, soil stratigraphy, or associated cultural artifacts.
Extraction_lab Facility where the DNA was actually extracted from the bone/tooth.
Number of reads Total raw count of DNA sequences generated by the sequencing machine.
Endogeneous Content Percentage of DNA that actually belongs to the specimen.
Nuclear Coverage The average number of times each position in the nuclear genome was sequenced.
Sex The biological sex of the individual (XX/XY).
mtDNA Coverage The average number of times the mitochondrial genome was sequenced.
N_SNP_call The total count of specific genetic markers (SNPs) successfully identified.

3. Do the variables have units? Which one?

#Yes, some variables have units.
Variable Unit
Long, Lat Decimal degrees
Age Years
Number of reads Number of reads
Radiocarbon date Years
Percentage of endogenous %
Nuclear coverage x-fold coverage
N_SNP_call Number SNP
mtDNA Coverage x-fold coverage

4. Does the data set have a long format?

# No, it has a wide format as multiple measurements of a single observation are stored in a single row.

5. Read the excel file into R

In this step, we upload the data of the Excel file and we ensure the names of the columns stay as the header while deleting the first informative row:

excel_route <- "aao4776_tables1.xlsx" # The .xlsx file is uploaded with the task, so you may need to modify the file path depending on where you save it.
df <- read_excel(excel_route, skip = 1)
head(df)
## # A tibble: 6 × 21
##   Extract_ID Depository_ID        `Dog/Wolf Status` Country `Site name`         
##   <chr>      <chr>                <chr>             <chr>   <chr>               
## 1 JBG43      11S1232 I15 PP4747-1 Dog               USA     Janey B. Goode, Ill…
## 2 JBG41      11S1232 7892-1       Dog               USA     Janey B. Goode, Ill…
## 3 JBG17      11S1232 2601-1       Dog               USA     Janey B. Goode, Ill…
## 4 JBG11      11S1232 1671-1       Dog               USA     Janey B. Goode, Ill…
## 5 JBG42      11S1232 F9 PP4407-1  Dog               USA     Janey B. Goode, Ill…
## 6 JBG13      11S1232 939-1        Dog               USA     Janey B. Goode, Ill…
## # ℹ 16 more variables: `Analysed\nNuclear` <dbl>, `mtDNA \nAnalysed` <dbl>,
## #   `mtDNA capture` <dbl>, Extraction_lab <chr>, Lat <dbl>, Long <dbl>,
## #   `Radiocarbon Lab #` <chr>, `Direct date?` <chr>,
## #   `Radiocarbon date \n (95.4% interval, lab number)` <chr>,
## #   `Age \n(from context)` <chr>, `Number \nof reads` <dbl>,
## #   `Endogeneous\n Content` <dbl>, `Nuclear\nCoverage` <chr>, Sex <chr>,
## #   `mtDNA \nCoverage` <dbl>, N_SNP_call <chr>

6. Reshape the data if necessary in long format

We can see that we already have a long format, we have 98 rows for 98 observations and 21 columns for 21 variables.

str(df)
## tibble [98 × 21] (S3: tbl_df/tbl/data.frame)

sapply(df, class)

7. Check the variable classes and update them if necessary

We can also see that some variable classes are wrongly adjusted, per example the Dog status is stated as a character or we can see binary variables as numeric.

# We convert the "NA" in actual NA
df[df == "NA"] <- NA
df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    
    extract_id = as.character(extract_id),
    depository_id = as.character(depository_id),
    site_name = as.character(site_name),

    
    dog_wolf_status = as.factor(dog_wolf_status),
    country = as.factor(country),
    extraction_lab = as.factor(extraction_lab),
    sex = as.factor(sex),

    analysed_nuclear = as.factor(analysed_nuclear),
    mt_dna_analysed = as.factor(mt_dna_analysed),
    mt_dna_capture = as.factor(mt_dna_capture),
    direct_date = as.factor(direct_date),
    
    lat = as.numeric(lat),
    long = as.numeric(long),
    number_of_reads = as.numeric(number_of_reads),
    endogeneous_content = as.numeric(endogeneous_content),
    nuclear_coverage = as.numeric(nuclear_coverage),
    mt_dna_coverage = as.numeric(mt_dna_coverage),
    n_snp_call = as.numeric(n_snp_call)
    )

Finally, we can see our new data frame

df

## #   direct_date <fct>, radiocarbon_date_95_4_percent_interval_lab_number <chr>,
## #   age_from_context <chr>, number_of_reads <dbl>, endogeneous_content <dbl>,
## #   nuclear_coverage <dbl>, sex <fct>, mt_dna_coverage <dbl>, n_snp_call <dbl>

Part B

1. Represent the distribution of the variables: pick one continuous variable and one discrete variable and use histograms or bar graphs to show their distribution

library(ggplot2) # Needed to use ggplot function

# We have changed some columns names to avoid problems 

colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Extract_ID"] <- "extract_id"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Depository_ID"] <- "depository_id"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Dog/Wolf Status"] <- "dog_wolf_status"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Country"] <- "country"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Site name"] <- "site_name"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Analysed\nNuclear"] <- "analysed_nuclear"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "mtDNA \nAnalysed"] <- "mt_dna_analysed"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "mtDNA capture"] <- "mt_dna_capture"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Extraction_lab"] <- "extraction_lab"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Lat"] <- "lat"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Long"] <- "long"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Radiocarbon Lab #"] <- "radiocarbon_lab_number"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Direct date?"] <- "direct_date"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Radiocarbon date \n (95.4% interval, lab number)"] <- "radiocarbon_date_95ci"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Age \n(from context)"] <- "age_from_context"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Number \nof reads"] <- "number_of_reads"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Endogeneous\n Content"] <- "endogeneous_content"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Nuclear\nCoverage"] <- "nuclear_coverage"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Sex"] <- "sex"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "mtDNA \nCoverage"] <- "mt_dna_coverage"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "N_SNP_call"] <- "n_snp_call"

Plot 1 -> Number of Reads (Continuous) & Country (Discrete)

Helpful for visualizing how sequencing depth varies across different countries or geographic regions.

ggplot(df, aes(x = number_of_reads,fill = country)) + geom_histogram(bins = 30,color = "white") + scale_x_log10() + facet_wrap(~country) +scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Pastel2") + labs(title = "Distribution of reads in each Country", x ="Number of reads", y= "Count") + theme_get() +theme(legend.position = "none")

Plot 2 -> Endogenous Content (Continuous) & Dog/Wolf Status (Discrete)

Allows comparison of ancient DNA preservation quality between dog and wolf samples.

ggplot(df, aes(x = dog_wolf_status, y = endogeneous_content,fill = dog_wolf_status)) + geom_boxplot(color = "black", alpha = 0.7, width = 0.6) +geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.4, show.legend = FALSE) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") + labs(title = "Endogenous content distribution", x ="Dog/Wolf Status", y = "Endogenous Content") + theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "none", panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(), plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14))

Plot 3 -> mtDNA Coverage (Continuous) & Sex (Discrete)

Enables assessment of whether mitochondrial DNA coverage differs between male and female specimens.

ggplot(df, aes(x = mt_dna_coverage, fill = sex)) +geom_histogram(bins = 10, position = "dodge", color = "white") + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Pastel1",na.value = "gray", na.translate =FALSE) + labs(title = "mtDNA Coverage distribution by Sex", x = "mtDNA Coverage", y = "Sex") + theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position ="bottom", plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14))

Plot 4 -> Latitude (Continuous) & Country (Discrete)

Visualizes the latitudinal position of samples categorized by their country of origin.

df_summary <- df %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  summarize(mean_lat = mean(lat, na.rm = TRUE))

ggplot(df_summary, aes(x = country, y = mean_lat, fill = country)) + geom_col(width = 0.5, color = "black") + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") + labs(title = "Latitude in each Country", x = "Country", y = "Latitude") + theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "none", axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1), aspect.ratio = 0.5)

Plot 5 -> N_SNP_call (Continuous) & mtDNA Capture (Discrete)

Visualizes how the number of SNP calls varies depending on whether mtDNA capture was performed (1=Yes, 0=No).

df_plot5 <- df %>%
  group_by(mt_dna_capture) %>%
  summarize(
    mean_snp = mean(n_snp_call, na.rm = TRUE),
    se = sd(n_snp_call, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()))

ggplot(df_plot5, aes(x = mt_dna_capture, y = mean_snp, fill = mt_dna_capture)) + geom_col(width = 0.5, color = "black", alpha = 1) + geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_snp - se, ymax = mean_snp + se), width = 0.2) + scale_y_log10(labels = scales::label_comma()) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") + labs(title = "N SNP call and mtDNA Capture", x = "mtDNA capture", y = "N SNP call") + theme_minimal() + theme(legend.position = "none", plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14), aspect.ratio = 0.5)

Plot 6 -> Endogenous Content (Continuous) & Country (Discrete)

Visualizes if DNA preservation quality correlates with geographic location.

ggplot(df, aes(x = endogeneous_content, fill = country)) + geom_density(alpha = 0.4, color = "white", na.rm = TRUE) + scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") + labs(title = "Endogenous Content distribution in each country", x = "Endogenous Content", y = "Density", fill = "Country") + theme_minimal() + theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14), panel.grid.minor = element_blank(), legend.position = "bottom", aspect.ratio = 0.4)

# Wanted to try a different kind of plot (Density Plot), information/help found in: https://r-graph-gallery.com/21-distribution-plot-using-ggplot2.html

2. Summarize the data: use one geom to summarize data (e.g.: geom_smooth, boxplots, …) of two variables

We have changed some columns names to avoid problems.

Plot 7 -> Numbers of reads & Dog/Wolf status

This plot summarizes the distribution of the number of reads for each animal type (dog or wolf) using a boxplot

colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Number \r\nof reads"] <- "number_of_reads"
colnames(df)[colnames(df) == "Dog/Wolf Status"] <- "dog_wolf_status"

library(scales)
ggplot(df, aes(x = `dog_wolf_status`, y = number_of_reads, fill = `dog_wolf_status`)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.3, color = "black")+ #used to see the content of each animal 
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6, outlier.shape = NA)+ #remove outliers because jitter is already doing it 
  scale_y_log10(labels = comma)#converts the numbers into a more legible format +

  labs(title = "Comparison of Reads Between Wolves and Dogs", x = "Wolf or dog status", y = "Number of reads",fill="Animal type")+
  theme_minimal()+
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold", hjust = 0.1),  #smaller tittle and position towards left
  )
## NULL

Plot 8 -> Number of Reads vs Endogenous Content

This plot indicates if samples with higher endogenous content have more reads

library(scales)
ggplot(df, aes(x = `endogeneous_content`, y = number_of_reads)) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.5)+ #Draws every observation as a point 
    geom_smooth() +
    scale_y_log10(labels = scales::comma) +
    labs(
      title = "Number of Reads vs Endogenous Content",
      x = "Endogenous Content (%)",
      y = "Number of Reads",
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold", hjust = 0.1)
    )
## `geom_smooth()` using method = 'loess' and formula = 'y ~ x'

3. Explain your data with graphics and text:

1. Choose the three graphics that better describe your data.

2. Customize and annotate them.

3. Accompany the figures with your hypothesis and/or interpretation.

1st graphic : Number of reads by country

ggplot(df, aes(x = number_of_reads,fill = country)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30,color = "white") +
  scale_x_log10() + facet_wrap(~country) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Pastel2") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Sequencing Depth by Country",
       subtitle = "Number of reads shown on log scale",
       x ="Number of reads (log10) ", y= "Count") +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11),
      strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")) + 
      annotate("text",
           x = 1e6,
           y = 8,
           label = "Right-skewed distribution",
           size = 3.5)

Hypothesis:

We hypothesize that number of reads may vary across countries due to differences in sample preservation conditions, excavation context, and laboratory processing strategies.

Interpretation:

The distribution of read counts is highly right-skewed across all countries, even after log transformation. The USA shows greater variability and a wider range of sequencing depth compared to other countries, likely reflecting differences in sample size and sequencing effort. These patterns suggest that technical and sampling factors may play a substantial role in shaping sequencing output.

2nd graphic : Endogenous Content by Dog/Wolf Status

ggplot(df, aes(x = dog_wolf_status, y = endogeneous_content,fill = dog_wolf_status)) +
  geom_boxplot(color = "black", alpha = 0.7, width = 0.6) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, alpha = 0.4, show.legend = FALSE) + 
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") + 
  labs(title = "Endogenous DNA Content by Species Status",
       x ="Dog/Wolf Status", y = "Endogenous Content") +
  theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11),
      strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")) +
  annotate("text",
           x = 1.25,
           y = 75,
           label = "Higher variability\nin dogs",
           size = 3.5)

Hypothesis:

We hypothesize that endogenous DNA content will not differ substantially between dogs and wolves, as DNA preservation is primarily influenced by environmental and burial conditions rather than taxonomic status.

Interpretation:

The distribution of endogenous content shows greater variability in dogs compared to wolves. While dogs appear to exhibit a higher median endogenous content, the wide dispersion and unequal sample sizes suggest caution in interpretation. The broader range observed in dogs may reflect differences in archaeological context, preservation environments, or sampling intensity rather than intrinsic biological differences between species.

3rd graphic : Endogenous Content by Country

ggplot(df, aes(x = endogeneous_content, fill = country)) + 
  geom_density(alpha = 0.4, color = "white", na.rm = TRUE) + 
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set1") + 
  labs(title = "Endogenous Content distribution in each country",
       x = "Endogenous Content", y = "Density", fill = "Country") + 
  theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11),
      strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")) +
  annotate("text",
           x = 40,
           y = 0.005,
           label = "Considerable overlap\nbetween countries",
           size = 3.5)

Hypothesis:

We hypothesize that endogenous DNA content may vary geographically due to differences in climatic conditions, burial environments, and site-specific taxonomic processes.

Interpretation:

The density distributions suggest some geographic variation in endogenous content. Samples from Russia appear to cluster around intermediate values, while Canada and the USA show broader distributions including higher endogenous percentages. However, substantial overlap among countries indicates that geographic location alone does not fully explain variation in DNA preservation. Environmental heterogeneity within countries and unequal sample sizes likely contribute to the observed patterns.

Part C

1. Make your figures interactive in three different ways (one for each figure):

- Make an interactive visualisation using some htmlwidget
- Add interactive controls with shiny input widgets to show some useful information (eg. focus on a subset, add a summary layer…). Use: modify what we see or filter the data.
- Make an interactive visualisation using shiny built-in interactive options in order to print the information of the individual observations. Use: making the plot itself interactive.
- Decide which figure would benefit from each type of interactivity and justify your choice
